behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the handler/verifier/queue handoff: the same request must both return `401` and suppress enqueue side effects. A pure unit test of signature math would miss forwarding bugs. Integration brake: no real OS/network/subprocess is required (in-process HTTP harness is enough), no AI output dependency, no multi-agent sequence. Rejection gate: rejected System because the behavior text is about `signature ... rejected with a 401` and `not forwarded`, which can be reproduced with in-process module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Run the HTTP handler in-process with a real signature-check module and a queue adapter test double. Send a body with an intentionally wrong `X-Signature-256`; assert `401` and assert no queue write occurred.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across modules (HTTP handler + persistence-backed queue). Minimum repro needs real module wiring and observable write-before-ack sequencing. Integration brake: no real external network/subprocess required; no AI dependency; no multi-step agent pipeline. Rejection gate: rejected System because behavior text requires `written to the persistent queue before ... 200`, not OS-level fault or real network behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process server plus a real local persistent queue implementation (e.g., sqlite/file-backed test instance). Instrument timestamps/events for `queue write committed` and `HTTP 200 sent`; assert write commit occurs first.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at async orchestration boundary: handler enqueue/dispatch path must decouple from downstream worker completion. A unit of one function can miss cross-module waiting. Integration brake: can be reproduced with in-process delayed worker stub; no real OS/network requirement, no AI/model output dependency, no agentic sequence. Rejection gate: rejected System because behavior text is specifically `returns ... without waiting`, which can be validated via in-process async wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler with a downstream processor that blocks on a controllable promise. Send request, assert HTTP response returns before releasing the processor promise, then release and verify downstream completion later.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: The behavior is explicitly wall-clock and concurrency under load. Real timing/scheduling effects (event loop contention, socket handling, queue pressure) are where failures surface; in-process fakes or mocked timers can hide them. Integration brake promotes to System due to real time/OS runtime behavior. Rejection gate: rejected Integration based on behavior text `within 3 seconds wall-clock` and `slow and concurrent`, which requires real runtime timing, not just module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service as a real process and send concurrent HTTP requests over localhost while earlier events trigger intentionally slow downstream work. Measure end-to-end response latency; assert each acknowledgment stays under 3 seconds.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is at module/data-store boundary (handler + idempotency check + queue write). A unit test of a local set/map does not prove persistence-backed dedupe behavior. Integration brake: no real OS/network/subprocess needed; no AI dependency; no multi-agent chain. Rejection gate: rejected System because behavior text only requires `ID was already received ... not written ... second time`, reproducible with local integrated components.
behaviors.test_description: Using in-process handler and real local persistence for idempotency state, submit the same event ID twice and assert only one queue record is created.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Behavior validates translation of queue backpressure signal into HTTP semantics (`503` + `Retry-After`) across handler/queue boundary. A single-function unit may miss mapping integration. Integration brake: queue-full condition can be injected by in-process queue implementation or stub; no required real OS/network/subprocess, no AI dependency, no multi-agent sequence. Rejection gate: rejected System because behavior text is about `queue reports it is full` and response mapping, not real infrastructure timing.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler in-process with queue module configured to return a `full` error/state. Assert response status is `503` and `Retry-After` header is present and correctly formatted.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This targets queue component behavior with real persistence interaction (capacity accounting and reject-on-overflow). Minimum repro is integrated queue + local store, not full system process/network. Integration brake: no real external network/subprocess needed; no AI/model-output dependence; no multi-step agent pipeline. Rejection gate: rejected System because behavior text focuses on queue enforcement logic (`enforces ... capacity limit`) rather than OS/network phenomena.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue against a local test store with capacity N, enqueue N events successfully, then enqueue N+1 and assert explicit rejection without state corruption.
